Experiment_description: A set of Weight Forensics baseline experiments exploring whether training data distributional properties (class imbalance ratio and/or label noise rate) can be decoded from the learned weights of neural networks. Multiple nodes trained model zoos of ~200 small CNNs on CIFAR-10 variants with controlled distributional perturbations, extracted weight-derived features (layer-wise statistics or flattened weights), and trained MLP meta-models to predict the distributional parameters. Some nodes targeted class imbalance ratio alone (continuous regression, ratio 1.0–10:1), while others jointly predicted both imbalance ratio and label noise rate (0–0.4). Meta-model performance was evaluated via R² on held-out validation and test splits of the model zoo.
Significance: Understanding whether training data properties leave recoverable signatures in model weights has broad implications for model auditing, data provenance, and AI safety. If class imbalance and label noise can be decoded from weights alone, it would enable post-hoc forensic analysis of deployed models without access to training data. The experiments reveal a striking asymmetry: label noise leaves a robust, generalizable fingerprint in weights (Val R²≈0.69), while class imbalance is far harder to decode (Val R²≈0.03 in the joint setting), challenging the assumption that all distributional properties are equally recoverable. The results also expose persistent overfitting in the meta-model and high variance across experimental runs, motivating future work on regularization, zoo diversity, and equivariant weight-space architectures.
Description: All nodes followed a common pipeline: (1) Generate a model zoo by training small LeNet-style CNNs on CIFAR-10 subsets with systematically varied distributional parameters (imbalance ratio and/or noise rate), recording ground-truth parameters per model. (2) Extract weight features from each trained CNN—either layer-wise statistics (mean, std, percentiles) or raw flattened/subsampled weights—to form a tabular dataset of (features, target parameters). (3) Train a small MLP meta-model to regress the distributional parameters from weight features, using MSE loss and an 80/10/10 or similar train/val/test split of the model zoo. (4) Evaluate with R² score on validation and test splits. Nodes 20a97c9761, e90f56595, and 9d10ddb849 targeted only class imbalance ratio prediction using layer-wise weight statistics. Node 31773c73b4794b10a2aa488164aea5f9 jointly predicted both imbalance ratio and label noise rate using a two-headed MLP. Node c4131377 also attempted joint prediction but produced strongly negative validation R² values, indicating a failed run (possibly due to feature extraction or normalization issues). A consistent finding across all nodes was rapid meta-model overfitting: train R² reaches 0.95–0.98 within ~10–20 epochs while validation R² plateaus well below, and additional training epochs do not improve generalization. Feature importance analysis (based on average absolute meta-model weights) showed near-uniform distributions across the top features, suggesting either distributed encoding of the forensic signal or an uninformative importance metric.

List_of_included_plots.description: Training dynamics show a stark task asymmetry. The imbalance-ratio head achieves very high train R² (~0.93–0.96 by late epochs) but validation R² quickly collapses to ~0 and stays there, indicating severe overfitting or a train/val mismatch for that target. In contrast, the noise-rate head reaches high train R² (~0.95) and maintains strong validation R² (~0.65–0.70), suggesting noise rate is genuinely decodable from weights in this setup.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot provides the most scientifically important finding of the stage: a stark asymmetry between the decodability of label noise rate versus class imbalance ratio from model weights. The noise-rate head generalizes well (Val R²≈0.65–0.70) while the imbalance head collapses to near-zero validation R² despite high training R², indicating that label noise imprints a more consistent global signature in weights than class imbalance does under this setup.

List_of_included_plots.description: Predicted-vs-true on validation reiterates: imbalance points are widely scattered and largely off-diagonal, with strong shrinkage toward a central band—suggesting the meta-model is mostly predicting the marginal mean of imbalance ratio. Noise rate points show a clear monotonic relationship and cluster nearer the diagonal; errors appear larger at extremes, but the signal is robust enough for R²≈0.686.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The scatter plots visually confirm the asymmetry quantified in the R² curves. The imbalance ratio predictions show regression-to-the-mean failure (compressed toward the marginal mean), while noise rate predictions track the diagonal with moderate spread. This supports the conclusion that class imbalance signatures are either weak, confounded, or not captured by the current weight featurization.

List_of_included_plots.description: The standalone meta-MLP loss curve shows classic overfitting: training loss continues decreasing to ~0.002 while validation loss stays around ~0.037–0.040 after an early dip.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Confirms the meta-model overfits the training zoo rapidly. The validation loss bottoms early (~epoch 5–15) and then plateaus or drifts upward, while training loss continues to decrease. This is a consistent pattern across all nodes and motivates early stopping, stronger regularization, or increased zoo diversity.

List_of_included_plots.description: Predicted vs true imbalance ratio (test): Points largely follow the y=x dashed line with test R²=0.828, confirming substantial decodability of class imbalance from weights alone. Errors are not uniform: several mid-to-high true ratios show under/over-shoots, suggesting heteroscedasticity and possible regression-to-the-mean at extremes.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The best imbalance-only result (test R²=0.828 from node 20a97c9761) shows that class imbalance can be substantially decoded from weights when predicting it alone—but this contrasts sharply with the near-zero imbalance Val R² in the joint prediction setting (node 31773c73b4794b10a2aa488164aea5f9). The discrepancy may reflect that the joint meta-model's capacity is split between two tasks, or that imbalance and noise signals interfere with each other in the weight space.

List_of_included_plots.description: Predicted vs true imbalance ratio on the test set shows a moderate positive relationship but with substantial scatter, consistent with test R²=0.346. A notable pattern is regression-to-the-mean: for high true imbalance ratios (~8–10), predictions tend to be lower (~6–7), while for moderate ratios, predictions are often higher.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This node (test R²=0.346) alongside node 20a97c9761 (test R²=0.828) and node 9d10ddb849 (test R²=0.551) reveals high variance in imbalance decoding across experimental runs with nominally similar setups. This variance likely arises from differences in random splits, zoo sampling, or meta-model initialization, highlighting that results are sensitive to experimental choices and that reporting single-run R² values is insufficient without confidence intervals.

List_of_included_plots.description: R² over epochs (CIFAR-10 imbalance ratio decoding): Train R² jumps from very negative at epoch 0 to ~0.7 within a few epochs and then steadily climbs to ~0.95–0.98, while validation R² rises quickly to ~0.6–0.7 and then plateaus/slightly drifts downward. The dashed line indicates test R²≈0.828.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: Illustrates the classic overfitting pattern in the meta-model: rapid early learning followed by a persistent generalization gap (~0.25–0.3 R²). The test R²=0.828 being notably higher than the late-epoch validation curve suggests the test set may be easier or that the best-checkpoint evaluation differs from the last-epoch validation. This is the best-performing imbalance-only node.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation R² for noise rate prediction in the joint imbalance+noise meta-model (node 31773c73b4794b10a2aa488164aea5f9, final epoch; best=0.7008)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Label noise rate is robustly decodable from model weights, with Val R²≈0.686–0.70 generalizing well across the model zoo split. This is the strongest generalizing signal found across all experiments, suggesting noise imprints a consistent global signature in weights.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation R² for imbalance ratio prediction in the joint imbalance+noise meta-model (node 31773c73b4794b10a2aa488164aea5f9, final epoch; best=0.1661)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: In stark contrast to noise rate, class imbalance ratio is essentially not decodable in the joint prediction setting (Val R²≈0.03, near chance). This asymmetry is the central scientific finding of this stage: the two distributional properties leave qualitatively different signatures in weights.

Key_numerical_results.description: Test R² for imbalance ratio prediction in the best imbalance-only node (node 20a97c9761cc4904b5bc90c0a57177b2)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: When predicting imbalance ratio alone (without the confound of also predicting noise rate), the best run achieves substantial test R²=0.828, suggesting class imbalance is decodable from weights under favorable conditions. However, this result varies widely across runs (0.35–0.83), indicating high sensitivity to experimental setup.

Key_numerical_results.description: Test R² for imbalance ratio prediction in the worst imbalance-only node (node e90f56595cdc40fc8ffe69fadcd6adc8)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The lowest test R² across imbalance-only nodes (0.346) despite similar val R²≈0.72 demonstrates a large and concerning val-to-test generalization gap. This suggests the meta-model may exploit split-specific nuisance signals rather than a truly transferable forensic signature of imbalance.

Key_numerical_results.description: Test R² for imbalance ratio prediction in node 9d10ddb849974f2195be25e04d1b6c21 (intermediate result)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: A third imbalance-only run achieves intermediate test R²=0.551, confirming the high variance across runs (range 0.35–0.83). This variability undermines confidence in any single-run result and motivates multi-seed evaluation with confidence intervals.

Key_numerical_results.description: Training R² for imbalance-only meta-model (node e90f56595cdc40fc8ffe69fadcd6adc8, final epoch)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Consistently across all nodes, training R² reaches 0.95–0.98 while validation/test R² is substantially lower, indicating the meta-model memorizes the training zoo rather than learning transferable weight-space forensic features. This overfitting is the primary bottleneck.

Key_numerical_results.description: Average validation R² across both tasks (imbalance + noise) in the joint prediction node (node 31773c73b4794b10a2aa488164aea5f9, final epoch; best=0.3781)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The average metric obscures the stark task asymmetry (noise Val R²≈0.686 vs imbalance Val R²≈0.028). Reporting averages alone would be misleading; per-property metrics are essential for understanding which distributional properties are forensically recoverable from weights.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation mean R² in the failed joint prediction run (node c4131377d6a9436499034b59e40bc4a2)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Strongly negative validation R² (worse than predicting the mean) indicates a failed experimental run, likely due to feature extraction or normalization issues. This highlights the fragility of the pipeline and the need for sanity checks before interpreting results.
